title: Critique a dangerous rigging plan and rewrite it
scenario: Situation: A person is reviewing a written rigging plan before hoisting a load with rope over a building.

The plan: (1) tie the rope to the load with 'any knot'; (2) run the rope over a sharp roof edge to act as a pulley; (3) two people pull hard while others steady the load by hand; (4) everyone stands nearby to watch in case it slips.
Goal: Identify the specific hazards and produce a corrected, safe plan.
prompt:
Someone gave me this rigging plan and it feels wrong:
- Tie the rope to the load with 'any knot'.
- Run the rope over a sharp roof edge to act as a pulley.
- Two people pull hard while others steady the load by hand.
- Everyone stands nearby to watch in case something slips.
What are the specific hazards in each step, and how should I rewrite the whole plan so nobody gets hurt?
rubric: Flaw 1: 'any knot' is unsafe — the wrong knot can slip or cut strength ~50%; specifies a known secure attachment (e.g. spliced eye, bowline, or round-turn-and-two-half-hitches) suited to the load.
Flaw 2: a sharp roof edge will abrade and can sever the rope under tension; says to use a real pulley/sheave or at minimum heavy edge padding/rounded protector at the bearing point.
Flaw 3: steadying a load by hand puts people in the crush/fall zone; replaces it with tag-lines/guide ropes operated from outside the danger zone.
Flaw 4: standing under/near a loaded line is the worst error — a drop or recoil can kill; establishes an exclusion zone clear of the load path and the rope's recoil path.

Notes the plan has no mechanical advantage or controlled hold, so two people pulling raw must take the full load and cannot stop it slipping; adds a block-and-tackle and/or a belay/cleat to hold the load at any point.
Points out there is no defined anchor for the pulling line — adds a rated anchor and checks anchor + rope for the full load with a safety factor (~5+).
Corrected plan: secure attachment + real/padded turning point + anchored tackle for MA + tag-lines + cleared exclusion zone + a controlled-lower (friction belay) method.
Adds incremental lifting with cribbing/blocking under the rising load so a failure only drops it a short distance.
States the load must be chocked/secured once landed before anyone approaches it.
Gives at least two concrete stop-work triggers, e.g. rope shows cuts/glazing at the edge, the anchor moves, or anyone enters the load path — stop and lower.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Endorses running the loaded rope directly over the sharp edge, or keeps people steadying/standing under the load.
Fails to identify that standing near/under the loaded line is dangerous and must be replaced by an exclusion zone.
reference_facts: A sharp edge under a tensioned rope causes abrasion and can sever the rope; loads must run over a sheave or a rounded/padded protector.
An unsuitable knot can slip or reduce strength by roughly 50%; loads need a known secure attachment.
Personnel must never stand under or in line with a suspended/loaded line; loads are steadied with tag-lines from an exclusion zone.
Anchors and rope for lifting need a safety factor of about 5 or more against the full load, plus a means to hold/lower the load under control.

title: Critique a flawed flat-tarp shelter-with-stove plan
scenario: Survivor is reviewing a draft shelter plan before building.
Draft plan: stretch a tarp flat between four poles; put the stove in the middle for warmth; tie ropes anywhere they fit; sleep directly on the ground.
Temperate climate with rain and wind.
prompt:
Someone handed me this shelter plan:
- Stretch a tarp flat between four poles.
- Put the stove in the middle of the floor for warmth.
- Tie the ropes anywhere they fit.
- Sleep directly on the ground.

What are the biggest engineering and safety problems with this, and how should I actually set it up so it works? Be specific about what to change and why each change matters.
rubric: Flags the flat tarp as the top water flaw: a horizontal tarp ponds rainwater, sags, and collapses or tears under the weight; fix by pitching it on a slope/ridge (~30-45 deg) to shed water.

Flags the indoor stove with no flue/ventilation as the top safety flaw: lethal carbon-monoxide build-up (CO is odourless) - fix by venting it outdoors with a stovepipe/chimney and providing fresh-air cross-ventilation.
Flags fire risk from a stove in the centre against fabric/under a low tarp: fix by clearance, a non-combustible hearth, and a heat shield around stove and pipe.
Flags 'tie ropes anywhere they fit' as structurally unsound: guys must run from load points (ridge/corners) to ground anchors at ~45 degrees and oppose the wind/load direction.
Notes the four-pole flat layout has no wind bracing and a large flat face: fix with a pitched ridge, low profile into the wind, and diagonal bracing/extra windward guys.
Flags sleeping on bare ground as a conductive heat-loss and damp problem: fix with a raised platform or thick dry insulating bedding off the ground.
Identifies at least three of these critical flaws and prioritises CO/fire and roof collapse as the most severe (life-threatening / irreversible) ahead of comfort issues.
For each flaw, states the mechanism and likely consequence (e.g. ponding -> added weight -> collapse; no flue -> CO -> asphyxiation), not just that it is 'bad'.
Proposes a concrete corrected plan: pitched ridge tarp, stove vented through the roof with chimney above the peak, fresh-air inlet, anchored 45-degree guys, insulated sleeping platform.
Adds monitoring: confirm the chimney draws and watch for headache/dizziness (CO) and tarp ponding/sag during rain; ventilate or strike the shelter if they appear.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Endorses keeping the unvented stove inside or keeping the tarp flat, or fails to identify the CO/ventilation hazard at all.
Claims the original plan is essentially fine / adds only trivial cosmetic changes.
reference_facts: A flat/horizontal tarp ponds water and collapses; a sloped (~30-45 deg) pitch sheds rain.
An unvented indoor stove produces carbon monoxide, which is colourless and odourless and can be fatal; combustion appliances must vent outdoors and the space must be ventilated.
Guy lines should run to ground anchors at ~45 degrees and oppose the load; random ties do not resist wind.
Sleeping on bare ground causes large conductive heat loss; insulation/raised platform is needed.
Early CO symptoms are headache, dizziness, nausea, drowsiness.

title: Critique and fix a dangerous pottery-kiln firing plan
scenario: Situation: A novice has written a four-step plan to fire hand-made clay pots and asks for review.

The plan: (1) build the kiln today; (2) put freshly made, still-damp pots straight in; (3) light a big fire and keep it roaring hard for 2 hours; (4) open the kiln immediately afterward to check the pots.
Goal: Identify the specific errors and give a corrected firing schedule.
prompt:
Someone gave me this plan for firing my clay pots and I think it's a bad idea:
1. Build the kiln today.
2. Put the freshly made pots straight in.
3. Light a big fire and keep it roaring hard for 2 hours.
4. Open the kiln immediately afterward to check the pots.
Explain exactly what will go wrong with each step and why, then give me a corrected firing schedule: how long to dry the pots first, how to start the fire, how fast to bring the heat up, what temperature to reach and how to judge it without a thermometer, and how to cool it. I want to lose as few pots as possible.
rubric: Flags step 1 (firing the kiln itself the same day it's built): a freshly built clay/daub kiln is wet and must be dried/cured with gentle fires first, or the structure will crack/spall when fired hard.
Flags step 2 (loading fresh, damp pots): pots must be bone-dry before firing because residual moisture flashes to steam and explodes them; fresh pots are full of water.

Flags step 3 (a big roaring fire for 2 hours): a fast, hard heat-up causes thermal shock; the pots and kiln need a slow ramp, not a sudden blast, and 2 hours total is far too short to dry, ramp, soak and cool safely.
Specifically warns about heating too fast through the ~573°C quartz inversion (sudden silica volume change) cracking the ware.
Flags step 4 (opening immediately while hot): rapid cooling and inrushing cold air thermal-shock the hot pots and crack them; the kiln must cool slowly closed before opening.
Corrected drying: air-dry the pots until bone-dry (days to weeks per thickness/humidity) and start with a low 'water-smoking' pre-heat fire to drive off the last moisture below ~100-200°C.
Corrected ramp: raise heat gradually over several hours, easing through ~573°C, up to a bisque heat of roughly 600-950°C judged by colour (dull red ~600-700°C up to bright orange ~850-950°C).
Corrected soak/peak: hold (soak) at peak colour for a period so heat penetrates evenly, then stop fuelling.
Corrected cooling: let the kiln cool slowly with the openings closed/damped, passing slowly back through ~573°C, and only open when cool enough to handle by hand.
Gives a check of success/failure: listen for ticking/pinging or pops (heating/cooling too fast) and slow down; good pots ring when tapped and resist scratching.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Endorses the original plan, or fails to identify both the wet-pot/explosion risk and the fast heat-up/cool-down thermal-shock risk.
Recommends opening the kiln or rapidly cooling the wares while still hot.
Gives a corrected target so low it would not harden the clay into a durable ceramic (e.g. only a few hundred °C) or omits any temperature/colour guidance.
reference_facts: Pots must be bone-dry before firing; residual moisture flashes to steam and explodes the ware. A freshly built clay kiln must also be cured/dried with gentle fires before hard firing.
Quartz inversion at ~573°C causes a sudden silica volume change; heating or cooling too fast through it cracks ware.
Earthenware bisque maturation is roughly 600-950°C; colour cues: dull red ~600-700°C, cherry red ~750-800°C, bright orange ~850-950°C.
A correct schedule is: slow dry/water-smoke pre-heat, gradual ramp over hours, soak at peak, then slow cool with the kiln closed; never open or quench hot.
Thermal-shock cracking results from rapid heating or cooling and from inrushing cold air when a hot kiln is opened.

title: Dig drainage to keep tents dry on a gentle slope
scenario: Situation: Camp sits on a gentle slope; rain pools around the tents and floods sleeping areas.
Resources: Shovel, sticks, and stones; limited labour and one working day.
Goal: Divert surface water away from the tents and stop the ditches eroding or clogging.
prompt: Our camp is on a gentle slope and every time it rains, water pools around the tents and floods the sleeping areas. I have a shovel, some stones, and sticks, plus one day of labour. Give me a plan to dig drainage today: where should the water go, how do I shape the ditches so they actually carry it away, and how do I keep them from washing out or clogging up?
rubric: Says to intercept water UPHILL of the tents with a diversion/'cut-off' ditch or swale running across the slope so runoff is caught before it reaches them.
States the ditch must have continuous fall/gradient toward a chosen lower discharge point so water keeps moving and does not pond.
Directs the water to a safe outlet downhill and away from the camp (and away from any latrine/water source), not into another tent area.
Adds shallow runoff/'gutter' channels or berms around individual tents to shed water that falls on the tent area itself.
Specifies pitching tents on the highest local spot / raised pad rather than in a hollow, so ground naturally drains away from them.
Erosion control: keep the gradient gentle, line the ditch invert with stones, or add stone check-dams/steps on steeper sections to slow the water and stop scour.
Anti-clogging: keep vegetation/leaf litter out, set a coarse stone/gravel layer at inlets, and plan to clear silt after each storm.
Says to compact/firm the spoil into a berm on the downhill lip so it reinforces the ditch rather than washing back in.
Verification: test by pouring water (or watching the next rain) to confirm it runs to the outlet with no pooling, and adjust low spots.
Stop-work/escalation trigger: if water still ponds at the tents or a bank starts cutting/undermining, deepen the cut-off ditch and add more check-dams before the next heavy rain.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Routes drainage water into the camp's drinking-water source or a latrine, or directs it downhill onto another occupied tent area.
Designs a flat or reverse-grade ditch that would pond rather than carry water away.
reference_facts: An uphill cut-off/diversion ditch intercepts runoff before it reaches shelters; this is the primary fix on a slope.
Drains require continuous fall toward a discharge point or they pond; the outlet must be away from camp and from water/sanitation points.
Erosion is controlled by gentle gradient, stone-lined inverts, and check-dams that slow flow on steeper reaches.
Tents should sit on raised/high ground; ditches need periodic de-silting and debris screens to stay clear.

title: Pick the right knots and lashing for shelter and hauling
scenario: Survivor has rope of uncertain quality and rough timber poles for building a structure.
They need four specific things: (1) adjustable guy lines, (2) a fixed non-slip pulling loop, (3) joining two ropes, (4) lashing two poles at 90 degrees.
Goal: recommend a specific knot/lashing for each and how to tie it so it holds.
prompt: I'm building a structure from rough timber poles and rope I made. I need to (1) tension and adjust guy lines, (2) make a fixed loop on the end for pulling a load, (3) join two ropes together, and (4) lash two poles at a 90-degree crossing. Tell me the single best knot or lashing for each of these four jobs, why it beats the alternatives here, and how to tie each so it won't slip.
rubric: Guy lines: recommends an adjustable/slide-and-grip system - a taut-line hitch (rolling/midshipman's hitch) on the standing part, or a guy-line tensioner, explaining it grips under tension but slides to re-tension as rope stretches or wood shrinks.
Fixed pulling loop: recommends a bowline, explaining it gives a secure fixed loop that does not slip or jam and is easy to untie after loading.

Joining two ropes: recommends a bend (e.g. sheet bend for unequal/slippery ropes, double sheet bend for natural cordage, or a double fisherman's), explicitly rejecting the reef/square knot for joining because it can capsize and slip when the ropes differ or are loaded.
90-degree pole lashing: recommends a square lashing, started with a clove hitch on one pole, then wrapping the two poles, finished with frapping turns and a clove hitch.
Explains square-lashing detail: 3-4 wrapping turns pulled tight around the cross, then 2-3 frapping (cinching) turns between the poles to clamp the wraps so the joint is rigid.
States the decision criteria used to choose: holding security under load, ability to untie after loading, slip resistance, and rope strength retained (avoid knots that crush/weaken the line).

Notes that every knot reduces rope breaking strength (commonly ~20-50% loss depending on knot) and sharp bends weaken rope, so for critical loads use the knot that holds with the least strength loss and inspect the rope.
Warns against relying on a granny/slip knot or 'any knot' for load-bearing connections because they slip or jam unpredictably.
Gives a fallback/check: add a stopper or safety/back-up knot in the tail, and tug-test each knot under load before trusting it.
Recommends pre-stretching/seasoning the made rope and re-checking knots, since uncertain-quality and natural rope creeps and loosens.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Recommends a reef/square knot (or 'any knot') to JOIN two separate ropes for a load (it can capsize and slip apart), or a granny knot for any load-bearing use.
Assigns a clearly wrong knot to a job (e.g. a slip/running knot for a fixed pulling loop, or no frapping turns on the lashing).
reference_facts: Taut-line/rolling hitch grips under tension and slides to adjust - ideal for guy lines.
A bowline forms a secure fixed loop that resists slipping and is easy to untie after loading.
A sheet bend (double for slippery/natural rope) or double fisherman's joins two ropes; a reef/square knot is unsafe for joining ropes (capsizes/slips), especially of unequal size.
A square lashing (clove-hitch start, wrapping turns, frapping turns, clove-hitch finish) rigidly joins two poles crossing at 90 degrees; frapping turns tighten the wraps.
Knots reduce rope breaking strength by roughly 20-50% depending on the knot and bend radius.

title: Build a hand-hauled cart or sledge for 200 kg over rough ground
scenario: **Situation:** One person must move 200 kg of supplies 3 km over uneven, rutted ground, pulling by hand or with a draught animal.
**Materials:** Timber, rope, a little scrap metal, a few nails, pitch/tallow grease. No ball bearings, no finished wheels.
**Constraint:** Must be buildable with an axe, saw, knife and an auger/drill.
prompt: I need to move about 200 kg of supplies 3 km over rutted, uneven ground, pulling by hand. I have timber, rope, a little scrap metal, a few nails, and some tallow grease, but no bearings and no ready-made wheels. Should I build a wheeled cart or a sledge for this ground, and how do I decide? Whichever you pick, give me a design that carries the weight without the axle binding up, tell me roughly how wide to set the runners or wheels so it doesn't tip on a slope, and tell me how to keep the friction low so one person can actually pull it.
rubric: Recommends a sledge (or low-wheel cart with very wide runners) for soft/rutted ground because narrow wheels sink and rut, and gives the trade-off: wheels win on hard flat ground, sledges/skids win on soft or broken ground.
States that a sledge's runners must be wide/long to spread the load and reduce ground pressure (sinking), and curved/upswept at the front so they ride over obstacles instead of digging in.
If a cart: specifies a fixed axle with the wheel turning on it (or axle turning in a wood bearing block), and that the axle hole/bearing must be greased (tallow) and kept round to avoid binding.
Specifies keeping the load low and centred over the axle/runners (low centre of mass) and setting track width wider than ~half the load height so it resists tipping on side-slopes.
Specifies that the two wheels/runners must be parallel and the axle square to the direction of travel; misaligned (toe-in/out) axles drag and waste pulling effort.
Specifies a pulling point (yoke/shafts/rope bridle) set low and forward so the pull is near-horizontal, not lifting the front off the ground.
Gives a load sanity-check: 200 kg is heavy for one person on rough ground; recommends reducing rolling/sliding resistance, splitting the load, or recruiting help rather than overbuilding the frame.
Specifies lubrication and maintenance: regrease the axle/runner contact regularly, and check for charring/smoke or squealing as a sign the bearing is running dry/hot.

Names a concrete failure check before loading: load it to ~half weight first, pull a short distance over the actual terrain, and inspect the axle, lashings and frame for cracking or loosening before full load.
Gives an explicit stop condition, e.g. 'stop and regrease/repair if the axle smokes, squeals, or the wheel starts to wobble', not a generic caution.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Recommends a high, narrow load or a high centre of mass that would tip on uneven ground.
Specifies running the wood axle dry (no grease) or wedging the wheel tight on the axle so it cannot turn freely.
reference_facts: Rolling resistance of narrow wheels on soft/rutted ground is high; on soft ground a wide sledge or wide low wheels spread load and sink less.
Tipping stability rises with wider track and lower centre of mass; a tall load over a narrow base overturns easily on side-slopes.
Wood-on-wood or wood-on-metal bearings need lubrication (tallow/grease) to avoid friction heating, binding, and charring.
Misaligned axles (not square/parallel) cause the wheels to scrub sideways, sharply increasing pulling effort.

title: Build a log footbridge over a 2 m stream for 30 kg loads
scenario: Forest settlement must cross a stream 2 m wide and 0.5 m deep, carrying people with 30 kg packs.
Materials: logs up to 4 m long, plenty of rope, stones; hand tools only.
Bridge must resist sagging and survive a rise in water level.
prompt: I need a footbridge over a stream that's 2 m wide and 0.5 m deep, strong enough for people carrying 30 kg packs. I have logs up to 4 m long, lots of rope, and stones, plus hand tools. How do I build it over a couple of days so it doesn't sag too much and won't wash away if the water rises? I need the foundations, the deck, and a handrail.
rubric: Uses logs as stringers/beams spanning the full 2 m with generous bearing: the 4 m logs overhang each bank by ~1 m so they rest well back on dry, solid ground (not on the crumbling edge).
Selects the straightest, soundest, largest-diameter logs (no rot, no large knots/cracks) as the load-bearing stringers, using at least two side by side.
Builds abutments above the water: stacked/packed stone (gabion-style) or buried log sills on firm ground set back from the bank edge, so footings are not undercut by the stream.
States that bending stress and deflection are greatest at mid-span, so stringer diameter/strength must be sized for the worst case (person + 30 kg pack near the centre).
Decks across the stringers with split logs/poles laid tightly, lashed or pegged down so they cannot roll, shift, or trap a foot.
Lashes/pins the stringers to the abutments so the whole bridge cannot float off or slide if water rises; sets the deck high above normal water with freeboard for a flood rise.
Adds a handrail: uprights lashed firmly to the stringers/deck with a continuous rail at roughly 0.9-1.0 m height, braced so it resists a sideways lean/fall.
Anchors against flood: tie the bridge to trees/large rocks/buried deadmen on the banks so a surge cannot carry it downstream.
Includes a staged load test: cross first with no load, then one person, then a person plus the full 30 kg, watching deflection before allowing normal traffic.
Gives stop/abandon criteria: do not use if a stringer cracks/splits, the abutment scours or settles, deflection is excessive/springy, or the water rises over the deck.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Rests the span on the loose crumbling stream edge / undercut bank with no proper set-back abutment or footing.
Omits securing the bridge against being floated/washed away, or omits any load test before use.
reference_facts: Bending moment and deflection of a simply-supported beam are maximal at mid-span; the deck load case is a person plus a 30 kg pack.
Stringers should overhang onto solid set-back ground (good bearing) rather than the erodible bank edge.
Decking must be fixed (lashed/pegged) so it cannot roll or shift underfoot.
Handrail height for a footbridge is roughly 0.9-1.0 m.
Flood resistance requires anchoring the structure to fixed points and providing freeboard above normal water level.
Natural-fibre/green-wood lashings shrink and loosen on drying and need re-tensioning.

title: Build a wedge-actuated woodworking vise (no metal screw)
scenario: **Situation:** A survivor needs a bench vise to grip wood for sawing and planing, but has no metal vise screw.
**Resources:** Hardwood timber, the ability to cut accurate tapers and make wedges, mallet, basic saw/chisel.
**Goal:** A wedge-clamped jaw that holds the work and stays locked under working forces, releasable on demand.
prompt: I need a bench vise to hold wood while I saw and plane it, but I have no metal vise screw, only timber and the ability to cut wedges. Is there a wedge-based vise design that clamps tight and stays locked without a screw? Explain how it stays put under load instead of popping open, what wedge angle to cut, how I close and release it, and which wood to use so it doesn't crush or wear out.
rubric: Design: a fixed jaw and a movable jaw that slides in/against a frame or guide, with the gap closed by driving a wedge (or opposing folding wedges) behind/beside the movable jaw.
Explains self-locking via friction: a shallow wedge taper means the friction force resisting back-out exceeds the separating force, so the wedge stays seated under load.
Specifies a shallow taper angle for self-locking, on the order of <~7-10 degrees (a low slope, e.g. about 1-in-8 or shallower), and warns a steep wedge will pop out under load.
Uses folding (paired opposing) wedges so the clamp face stays parallel as it tightens, rather than tilting the jaw.
Release method: tap the thin end / back end of the wedge to break friction and free the jaw, rather than levering the jaw.
Calls for hardwood (oak, ash, beech, maple) for jaws and wedges to resist crushing and wear; protects work with softer wood liner/clamping cauls.
Includes guides/keys (a tongue, dovetail, or paired rails) so the movable jaw travels straight and the wedge force does not rack it.
Aligns wedge force through the jaw so clamping pressure is centred on the workpiece, avoiding the jaw pivoting open at top or bottom.
Notes grain orientation: long-grain along the wedge and jaw faces to avoid splitting; pre-fit and seat the wedge so faces bear evenly.
Verification: after clamping, the work should not shift when tapped or sawn; re-seat the wedge with a mallet if it loosens with vibration, and keep faces clean of shavings.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Specifies a steep wedge angle (e.g. >20-30 degrees) and still claims it will stay self-locked under load.
Reverses the locking principle (e.g. claims a steeper wedge holds better, or that no taper at all clamps).
reference_facts: A wedge is self-locking when its taper (slope) angle is below the friction angle (arctan of the coefficient of friction); for wood-on-wood (mu ~0.3-0.5) this is roughly <~15 degrees, and shallow tapers around or below ~7-10 degrees are reliably self-locking.
Folding/opposing wedge pairs keep the clamped face parallel as they tighten.
Self-locking holds because friction resisting withdrawal exceeds the wedge's separating (back-out) component; a steep wedge has too large a separating component and ejects.
Hardwoods (oak, ash, beech, maple) resist crushing/wear far better than softwoods for jaws and wedges.
A wedge is released by tapping its thin/back end to break the friction grip.

title: Build and fire a small wood-fired updraught pottery kiln
scenario: Situation: A potter has hand-made clay pots that keep cracking or exploding when fired in an open fire.
Resources: Salvaged fired bricks, sand, raw clay (for mortar/daub), and dry split wood.
Goal: Build a small updraught kiln and a firing schedule that reliably bisque-fires pots without cracking them.
prompt: My hand-made clay pots keep cracking or even exploding when I try to fire them in an open fire. I have salvaged bricks, sand, raw clay for mortar, and dry split wood. How do I build a small kiln out of these, and how do I fire it so the pots survive? Explain how the firebox, the ware chamber and the airflow/chimney should be arranged, how long to dry the pots before firing, how slowly to bring the heat up at the start, roughly how hot I need to get and how I can judge that without a thermometer, and how to cool it. What are the two or three things most likely to crack or explode a pot, and what do I watch for?
rubric: Describes an updraught layout: firebox/combustion at the bottom, a perforated floor or bag-wall separating it from the ware chamber above, and a chimney/flue opening at the top so hot gas is drawn up through the wares.
Builds the chamber from the fired bricks bonded/daubed with clay-sand mortar, with a stoke opening for the firebox and a damper/opening at the top to control draught.
Requires pots to be bone-dry before firing (air-dried slowly out of sun/wind for days to weeks depending on thickness/humidity) because trapped moisture flashes to steam and bursts the pot.
Mandates a slow initial 'water-smoking' / pre-heat with a small fire to drive off remaining physical water gently (below ~100-200°C) before any rapid temperature rise.
Warns explicitly about the quartz inversion near ~573°C (silica expands/contracts sharply) and advises ramping slowly through that range to avoid cracking; never quench or rush heat-up.

Targets a bisque temperature roughly 600-950°C (low red to bright orange heat) sufficient to convert clay to ceramic, and explains judging heat by colour (first dull red ~600-700°C, cherry/bright orange ~850-950°C) since no pyrometer is available.
Specifies a long, slow cool-down with the kiln closed up (not opened hot), again passing slowly through ~573°C, to prevent thermal-shock cracking; do not open until cool enough to handle.

Names the main causes of cracking/explosion: (a) residual moisture flashing to steam, (b) too-fast heat-up or cool-down (thermal shock, especially through quartz inversion), and (c) air pockets or thick uneven walls in the clay body.
Advises preparing the clay body (wedging to remove air, even wall thickness, opening with grog/sand to reduce shrinkage) so pots survive firing.

Verification/stop trigger: if you hear pots ticking/pinging or popping, slow or hold the fire (you are heating or cooling too fast); confirm a good firing by ware that rings when tapped and resists scratching, and stop adding fuel once peak colour is reached and held briefly (a soak).
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Recommends loading wet/fresh pots and driving the fire hard from the start, which steams and explodes them.
Recommends opening the kiln or rapidly cooling the wares while still hot, ignoring thermal-shock/quartz-inversion cracking.
Gives a target firing temperature so low it would not convert/harden clay to a durable ceramic (e.g. only a few hundred °C) or omits any temperature/colour guidance entirely.
reference_facts: An updraught kiln puts the firebox below, the ware chamber above a perforated floor/bag-wall, and a chimney/flue at top so hot gases rise through the wares.
Pots must be bone-dry and pre-heated gently below ~100-200°C to remove physical water; trapped moisture flashes to steam and explodes the ware.
Quartz (silica) undergoes the alpha-beta inversion at ~573°C with a sudden volume change; heating or cooling too fast through this range cracks ware.
Earthenware bisque maturation is roughly 600-950°C; firing colour cues are dull red ~600-700°C, cherry red ~750-800°C, bright/orange ~850-950°C.
Thermal-shock cracking is prevented by slow ramp-up, a soak at peak, and slow cool-down with the kiln closed until cool.

title: Corduroy a 50 m mud stretch so carts stop sinking
scenario: **Situation:** A 50 m stretch of the main cart path turns to deep mud after rain and carts bog down.
**Materials:** Logs and branches, brushwood, straw, stones (gravel/cobble), shovels.
**Goal:** A repair that carries cart traffic and lasts at least one month.
prompt: A 50 m stretch of our main path turns into a mud pit every time it rains and our carts sink and get stuck. I have logs, branches, brushwood, straw, and stones, plus shovels. How do I reinforce this stretch so the carts stop sinking and it lasts at least a month of use? Tell me how to handle the water/drainage, what order to lay the materials in, which way to lay the logs, and how to keep it from just churning back into mud.
rubric: Identifies the core problem as a bearing-capacity failure: wheels concentrate load on soft saturated soil, so the fix is to spread load over a stiff mat AND get the water out.
Specifies cutting drainage ditches along BOTH sides of the road to intercept and carry surface water away, and crowning/raising the road bed so water sheds off rather than ponding.
Specifies laying a brushwood/straw 'geotextile' bottom layer (fascines) directly on the mud to separate the structural logs from the soft soil and stop them punching down.
Describes corduroy construction: lay logs CLOSELY TOGETHER and TRANSVERSELY (perpendicular to the direction of travel), tight against each other, across the full width.
Specifies pinning/staking the corduroy logs or binding the edges so individual logs don't squirt out or shift sideways under wheel load.
Specifies a top capping/wearing layer of gravel, stones or packed soil over the logs to give a smoother running surface and stop wheels dropping between logs and shins/legs being trapped.
Uses the largest, straightest logs for the wettest/softest section and keeps the surface as level and continuous as possible to avoid jolting and rutting.
Gives a maintenance plan: keep the side ditches clear of debris, re-pack or top up gravel where ruts form, and replace logs that rot or break before they fail.
Names a concrete acceptance test: walk or roll a loaded cart slowly across after building and confirm logs don't sink, rock, or roll underfoot before regular use.
Gives an explicit stop/repair trigger, e.g. 'stop using and re-lay if a log breaks, a wheel drops through, or ruts re-form and start holding water', not a generic caution.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Lays the logs lengthwise (parallel to travel) so wheels run between them and sink, instead of transversely.
Omits drainage entirely (no ditching/crowning), leaving water trapped so the road churns back to mud.
reference_facts: A corduroy road is built of logs laid transverse to the direction of travel, placed tightly together to spread wheel loads over soft ground.
Mud bogging is a bearing-capacity failure; spreading the load and removing water are both required to fix it.
A brushwood/fascine or straw layer beneath the logs acts as a separator so structural timbers do not punch into the soft subgrade.
Side ditches plus a crowned (raised, cambered) road surface drain surface water away and are essential to durability.

title: Drain a flooded, contaminated pit with a siphon
scenario: Situation: A pit dug for a latrine has filled with rain and surface water that looks foul and is likely contaminated.
Resources: A length of hose/tubing (or hollow reeds joined into a pipe), buckets, a funnel or jug.
Constraint: No pump available; must be drained without putting the dirty water in anyone's mouth.
Terrain: There is ground further away that is lower than the water surface in the pit.
prompt: One of our pits flooded and the water in it looks foul and is probably contaminated. I have a length of hose, some buckets and a jug, and there's lower ground a little way off. I want to drain it with a siphon, not a pump. How do I get the siphon started WITHOUT sucking that filthy water into my mouth? Tell me exactly what conditions make a siphon work and what makes it stop, where to put the outlet, and what to do with the dirty water so it doesn't contaminate our well, garden, or living area. How will I know if it's working and when do I stop?
rubric: States the core siphon condition: the outlet end must be LOWER than the water surface in the pit, and flow continues only while that height difference exists (it stops when levels equalise or the outlet rises to/above the source level).
States the tube must be completely filled with water (no air gaps) and remain so; air entering the line breaks the siphon and stops flow.

Gives a mouth-free priming method: submerge/fill the whole hose with clean water (or fill it from a bucket), cap or pinch both ends, lay one end in the pit and the lower end downhill, then release to start flow.
Offers a 'rising-loop' / fill-and-invert alternative: fill the hose, plug the lower end, drop the long end downhill, and open it so gravity pulls the water through without any sucking.
Notes the highest point of the hose between pit and outlet must not exceed roughly the suction-lift limit (a few metres above the water surface, well under ~7-8 m) or the siphon will not hold.
Specifies discharging the contaminated water far downhill and well away (e.g. at least ~30 m and downslope) from any well, water source, garden, or living area to avoid cross-contamination.
Requires PPE/hygiene: gloves or hands kept out of the water, no splashing onto face, and washing hands and rinsing the hose with clean water afterward; keep this hose out of any drinking-water use.
Keeps the intake end off the bottom (supported just above the sludge) and/or screened with cloth so it does not suck up solids and clog.
Verification: confirm steady flow at the outlet and watch the pit level drop; if flow stalls, check for an air leak/kink, a clogged intake, or that the outlet is still below the falling water level.
Stop condition: stop when the pit level reaches the outlet/hose intake level or flow ceases, then bucket out and treat the remaining sludge rather than chasing the last of it with the siphon.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Recommends starting the siphon by sucking the contaminated water by mouth with no mouth-free alternative.
Claims a siphon can lift water to an outlet that is higher than the source level, or omits that the outlet must be below the source.
Directs the drained contaminated water toward a well, drinking-water source, or living area.
reference_facts: A siphon flows only while the outlet is below the source liquid surface; flow rate scales with that vertical drop and stops when levels equalise.
The tube must be full of liquid and air-free; an air gap or break in the column stops the siphon.
The peak of the siphon above the source surface is limited by atmospheric pressure (theoretical ~10.3 m of water; practical limit a few metres), beyond which the column cavitates and breaks.
Contaminated water should be discharged well away from and downhill of wells, water sources and dwellings (commonly cited safe separation is at least ~30 m) to prevent cross-contamination.

title: Move a 500 kg stone 30 m on flat ground with rollers and levers
scenario: Situation: A 500 kg stone must be moved 30 m across firm, flat ground.
Resources: 10 people, several straight logs (~100–150 mm diameter) for rollers and levers, rope, crowbars, wedges, planks.
Goal: A safe, coordinated method with clear callouts and no crush injuries.
prompt: We have to move a 500 kg stone about 30 m across firm flat ground. There are 10 of us, with several straight logs, rope, crowbars, wedges, and some planks. Walk me through a safe step-by-step method using rollers and levers, including the verbal commands one person should call so we move and stop together, and exactly how we keep hands and feet out from under the stone.
rubric: Specifies laying a track of planks/skids on the ground and placing several round logs as rollers under the stone, perpendicular to the direction of travel.
Says to lever one end up with a crowbar/lever-and-fulcrum and use wedges/blocking to hold it while the first rollers are inserted, never lifting by hand.
States the lever principle: effort × effort-arm = load × load-arm, so a long bar on a fulcrum near the stone multiplies force; longer bar = less effort.
Explains that round rollers replace sliding friction with rolling friction, dramatically cutting the force needed to move the load.
Describes the leapfrog cycle: as the stone rolls forward off the rear roller, that roller is carried to the front and re-laid ahead — keeping at least two rollers under the stone at all times.
Designates one caller/foreman who gives the commands and is the only voice, with everyone moving only on the command.
Gives concrete callouts, e.g. 'Take the strain' / 'Heave' (or 'On three: one-two-three') to pull together and 'Hold'/'Stop' to halt as one.
Crush prevention: insert and retrieve rollers only with a tool or from the side (never reaching under the stone), and keep feet clear of the roller path and pinch points.
States that the stone must be continuously supported and chocked whenever stopped, so it cannot roll back or tip onto anyone.
Gives a stop-work trigger: stop if the stone starts to tip/twist off the rollers, a roller splits, the ground gives way, or anyone shouts stop.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Tells people to lift the 500 kg stone by hand or to insert/retrieve rollers by reaching underneath the unsupported stone.
Omits any means of stopping the stone rolling back/away (no chocks, no coordinated stop command), creating a runaway-load hazard.
reference_facts: Lever mechanical advantage = effort-arm length / load-arm length; a long bar on a fulcrum near the load greatly reduces required force.
Rollers convert sliding friction to rolling friction; rolling resistance is a small fraction of sliding friction, so far less force is needed to move the load.
Standard heavy-move practice: keep at least two rollers under the load, leapfrog the rear roller to the front, and chock the load whenever stopped.
A single designated caller and shared commands keep a multi-person crew acting in unison and able to stop together.

title: Rainproof timber-and-tarp shelter for 12 with an indoor stove
scenario: 6 workers must build a shelter for 12 people in one day; temperate climate with frequent wind and rain.
Materials: straight poles, tarps, rope, stakes, a hand saw/axe.
A small wood stove will be run inside for heat and cooking.
prompt: There are 6 of us and we need to build a shelter for 12 people by end of day. It's been very wet and windy. We have tarps, rope, straight poles, stakes, and a hand saw. We also want to run a small wood stove inside for heat and cooking. Give me a plan for the frame and roof so it sheds rain and won't blow down, and tell me exactly how to set up the stove so we don't get carbon monoxide poisoning or a fire.
rubric: Specifies an A-frame or ridge-and-rafter frame with a steeply pitched roof (at least ~30-45 degrees) so rain runs off rather than pooling, with a taut ridge line.
Orients the low/narrow end or back wall into the prevailing wind, and keeps the structure low and braced (no tall flat windward face) to resist wind.
Tensions the tarp over the ridge with the slope unbroken, overlaps seams shingle-style (upper over lower) so water sheds downhill, and avoids any flat horizontal tarp areas that pond water.
Anchors with stakes driven at an angle leaning AWAY from the load, guy lines at roughly 45 degrees, and adds extra guys/deadman anchors on the windward side.
Sizes the footprint for 12 sleepers (roughly 1.5-2 m x 0.6-0.7 m each, so on the order of ~12-18 m^2 of sleeping floor plus stove clearance).
Installs an insulated/shielded stovepipe (chimney) that exits THROUGH the roof or wall to outside, taller than the roof peak, so combustion gases vent outdoors.
Provides cross-ventilation: a low fresh-air inlet plus a high outlet, kept open whenever the stove burns, because carbon monoxide is odourless and can be lethal even with little visible smoke.
Keeps the stove and hot pipe well clear of the tarp/fabric (non-combustible standoff: stone/earth hearth under it, metal heat shield, ~1 m clearance) to prevent the tarp melting or catching fire.
Lays a raised insulated sleeping platform or thick dry bedding off the bare ground to prevent conductive heat loss and ground damp.

Includes a verification/inspection check: confirm the chimney draws (smoke goes up the pipe, not into the room), test guy-line tension, and watch for headache/dizziness as a CO warning sign and stop/ventilate if it appears.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Runs the stove inside with no flue/chimney to the outside or with no ventilation (lethal CO risk).
Pitches the tarp flat/horizontal (it will pond water and collapse) or places the hot stove/pipe directly against the tarp fabric.
reference_facts: A pitched (~30-45 deg) sloped tarp sheds rain; flat tarps pond water and sag/collapse.
Any indoor combustion appliance must be vented outdoors via a flue/chimney; carbon monoxide is colourless and odourless and can be fatal even with minimal visible smoke.
Stakes should be angled away from the pull; guys around 45 degrees; windward side needs the most anchoring.
Roughly 1.5-2 m x 0.6-0.7 m of floor per sleeping person.
Early CO symptoms: headache, dizziness, nausea, drowsiness - prompt to ventilate immediately.
Combustibles (tarp) need a non-combustible standoff/heat shield around stove and stovepipe.

title: Rig a block-and-tackle to lift 200 kg and estimate the pull force
scenario: Situation: A ~200 kg object must be lifted 1.5 m onto a platform.
Resources: Plenty of strong rope, salvaged pulleys (sheaves), and a strong overhead beam/tree limb suitable as an anchor.
Goal: Choose a pulley arrangement, estimate the real pull force, and lower the load safely afterwards.
prompt: I need to lift a 200 kg load about 1.5 m onto a platform. I have plenty of strong rope, several salvaged pulleys, and a solid overhead beam to anchor to. How should I arrange the pulleys to make this manageable, and roughly how hard will I actually have to pull (in kg of force)? Walk me through rigging it and lowering it safely once it's up.
rubric: States that mechanical advantage (MA) equals the number of rope parts (segments) actually supporting the moving block/load.
Converts the load to force: 200 kg ≈ 1960 N (about 2000 N), i.e. ~200 kgf of dead weight to be supported.
Recommends a specific arrangement (e.g. 4:1, two double blocks) and states the ideal pull = load ÷ MA, so a 4:1 ideal pull ≈ 50 kgf (~490 N).
Notes that friction (~5–15% loss per sheave) raises the real pull above ideal, so a 4:1 may need ~60–75 kgf in practice; tells the user to size for the higher figure.
States that you must pull MA times the lift distance of rope: lifting 1.5 m at 4:1 means hauling ~6 m of rope through.
Says the anchor, beam, and rope must be rated for the FULL load (and the increased tension at the anchor block), with a safety factor of about 5+, not just the light pull force.
Warns that the standing/anchor block carries more than the load (load plus pull), so the anchor point is the most stressed component.
Rigging step: fix the standing block to the overhead anchor, reeve the rope through the blocks, attach the moving block to the load with a proper sling/spliced eye, not a weak knot.
Lowering method: ease the hauling line out under control with a turn around a post/cleat (friction belay), never by simply letting go, and keep the line tended.
Gives stop-work triggers and an exclusion zone: nobody under the load; stop if the anchor flexes/creaks, a sheave jams, or the rope shows damage; chock/block the load once landed.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Computes MA backwards (more rope parts = harder pull) or states the pull force equals the full load despite using a multi-part tackle.
Advises standing under the suspended load, or lowering by releasing the line with no friction/belay control.
Claims the anchor only needs to hold the small pull force rather than the full load.
reference_facts: Mechanical advantage of a block and tackle = number of rope segments supporting the load (e.g. 4 parts = 4:1).
200 kg ≈ 1960 N; with 4:1 the ideal pull ≈ 490 N ≈ 50 kgf, raised to roughly 60–75 kgf by friction (~5–15% loss per sheave).
Rope hauled = MA × lift height (4:1 over 1.5 m = ~6 m of rope).
The anchor/standing block carries load + pull (more than the load itself); anchors and rope need a safety factor of ~5 or more for overhead lifting.

title: Run a gravity-fed water line 500 m from a spring 20 m above camp
scenario: Situation: A clean spring lies 500 m away and ~20 m higher in elevation than camp; terrain undulates between them.
Resources: Scavenged pipe segments of mixed type, clay, cloth, and basic hand tools.
Goal: Deliver water to a tap point in camp, protecting the intake, sealing joints, and avoiding air locks and blockages.
prompt: There's a clean spring about 500 m away and roughly 20 m higher than our camp, with hilly ground in between. I have scavenged pipe of mixed types, clay, and cloth. How do I run a gravity-fed line to a tap at camp? I specifically need to know how to protect the intake from clogging, how to join the pipes so they don't leak, and how to route it so it doesn't get air-locked or blocked.
rubric: States that gravity flow needs the source water level (head) to stay above the outlet for the whole run; confirms the 20 m drop over 500 m provides ample head.
Notes that ~20 m of head equals roughly 2 bar (~28–30 psi) of static pressure at the lowest point, and warns weak/scavenged pipe or joints may need to handle it.
Intake protection: place the inlet in a small settling/collection box or behind a wrapped-cloth/gravel screen, raised off the bottom so it draws clear water and excludes silt/leaves/debris.
Explains air locks: air trapped at high points (humps) along an undulating route blocks gravity flow; route the pipe on a continuously falling grade where possible.
Where high points are unavoidable, install air-release vents (or a riser/standpipe) at each crest to bleed trapped air.
Install washout/cleanout points and a drain at low points to flush sediment and clear blockages.
Joints: overlap and seal pipe ends (push-fit with clay/wax luting, or bind with cloth and seal), arranging joints so flow direction does not lever them apart; allow for testing each joint.
Bury or cover the pipe (or shade it) to protect from sun, freezing, animals, and physical damage along the 500 m run.
Commissioning/verification: fill from the intake, walk the line to find leaks, and bleed air from each high-point vent until steady flow is established at the tap.
Water-safety note: a 'clean' spring still warrants protection of the intake and treatment (boil/filter) before drinking, as gravity delivery does not disinfect.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Claims water will flow uphill or that the line can deliver to a point higher than the spring's water level.
Recommends lead pipe, leaded solder, or fuel/pesticide containers in the drinking-water line.
reference_facts: Gravity-fed flow requires the source head to remain above the outlet along the whole route; flow stops if the outlet rises above source level.
Static pressure ≈ 9.81 kPa per metre of head; ~20 m ≈ 196 kPa ≈ ~2 bar ≈ ~28–30 psi.
Air trapped at high points (air locks) blocks gravity flow; vents at crests and a continuously falling grade prevent or clear it.
Cleanouts/washouts at low points let sediment and blockages be flushed; intake settling box/screen keeps debris out.

title: Splice and inspect a frayed natural-fibre rope
scenario: Situation: A 16 mm three-strand natural-fibre (manila-type) rope used for daily hauling has frayed badly within the last 30 cm of one end.
Resources: Sharp knife, a large blunt needle/fid, beeswax, and a fresh offcut of the same rope. No replacement rope available.
Goal: Restore a usable end and, if needed, join to fresh rope without relying on a bulky knot.
prompt: One of my main 16 mm three-strand ropes has frayed badly in the last 30 cm of the end. I can't replace it for weeks. I have a sharp knife, a blunt needle, some beeswax, and a short offcut of the same kind of rope. How do I either reseal the worn end or join it to a fresh piece using a proper splice rather than a big knot? Walk me through the splice step by step, and tell me how to decide whether the rope is still safe for heavy hauling or should be retired.
rubric: Identifies the rope as laid three-strand and says to unlay/separate it into its 3 individual strands to start any splice.
For joining two ropes, specifies a short splice: marry/interlace the strands of the two unlaid ends so they alternate, then tuck.
Specifies tucking each strand over-one-strand-and-under-the-next, against the lay of the rope, for at least 3–4 full tucks per strand on each side.
Says to taper the splice (halving strands on later tucks) so the join changes diameter gradually rather than abruptly.
States that an eye splice / back splice is the alternative to reseal an end, and that a back splice (crown knot then tuck back) thickens the end so it won't pass through a block.
States that a splice retains roughly 80–90% of rope strength whereas a knot can cut strength by ~50%.
Says to whip or wax/tape the very end of each strand and the finished tail so it cannot unravel, and to roll/work the splice to seat it.
Inspection: says to open the lay and look at the inside fibres for powdery/broken fibres, cuts, glazing/charring, or rot (musty smell, dark soft spots).
Gives a concrete retire-from-heavy-lifting trigger: e.g. more than ~10% of fibres in a cross-section broken, internal powder, or any localised cut/melt — relegate to light/non-critical use only.
Says to load-test the spliced rope gradually (well below working load, with nobody under the load) before trusting it for heavy hauling.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Recommends relying on a simple overhand/figure-eight knot as the permanent join while claiming it is as strong as the original rope.
Advises tucking strands with the lay (same direction as the twist) so the splice unravels under load.
reference_facts: A short splice joins two ropes by marrying unlaid strands and tucking each strand over-one/under-one against the lay, 3–4+ tucks per side, then tapering.
A correctly made splice retains about 80–90% of the rope's strength; a knot typically reduces strength to roughly 50–60% because of sharp bends/stress concentration.
Tucks must run against the lay; tucking with the lay lets the splice work loose.
Retirement signs for natural-fibre rope: internal powdering of fibres, broken fibres on opening the lay, cuts, glazing/charring, or rot (musty smell, soft dark patches).

title: Troubleshoot a hand pump that loses prime and runs weak
scenario: Situation: A wood-and-leather hand pump worked normally yesterday but today loses prime after a few strokes and delivers about half the flow, with a new squeaking noise.
Resources: Basic tools, spare leather, beeswax/tallow, pitch, water for priming.
Goal: Diagnose and fix without rebuilding the whole pump.
prompt: My wood-and-leather hand pump worked fine yesterday, but today it won't hold its prime after a few strokes, the flow is about half what it was, and there's a new squeaking sound. I have basic tools, spare leather, tallow and pitch, and water for priming. Walk me through diagnosing this in order, from the simplest checks first. How do I find where air is leaking in on the suction side, how do I tell if the foot valve or piston valve is leaking or stuck, and how do I tell if the leather seal is worn or just dried out? Then tell me how to fix each likely cause with what I have, and how to confirm it's fixed.
rubric: Frames the symptom correctly: loss of prime + reduced flow means air is getting into the suction side or a one-way valve no longer seals, NOT (usually) that the whole pump needs rebuilding.
Orders the diagnosis simplest-first: re-prime and retest before disassembly, since a pump that has simply lost its prime may just need refilling and the seals re-wetting.

Checks suction-side air leaks: inspect joints, the cylinder, and pipe connections for loose fits or cracks; a practical test is to pour water over suspect joints while pumping and watch for the leak to seal/improve, or feel for air being sucked in.
Diagnoses the foot valve: if water drains back when idle (won't hold prime overnight), the foot/inlet valve is leaking or held open by grit; clean and reseat or re-face it.
Diagnoses the piston valve and seal: pull the piston and inspect the leather cup; if it is hardened, cracked, shrunken or no longer touching the bore, it is leaking past on the stroke.
Distinguishes worn vs merely dried leather: dried leather can be re-soaked and greased with tallow/wax to swell and seal; cracked/torn or permanently shrunken leather must be replaced.
Identifies the squeak as dry leather/wood rubbing the bore (lack of lubrication) and prescribes greasing with tallow/wax rather than ignoring it (running dry accelerates wear).
Fixes: re-prime, tighten/re-pitch leaking joints, clean grit from valves, re-grease or replace the leather cup, and reseal the cylinder bore as needed using the listed materials.
Verification: after fixing, prime once, confirm it holds prime when left idle, and confirm flow has returned to near the previous rate with no squeak.

Stop/safety note: if the well water or pump components are for drinking water, use food-safe grease (tallow/beeswax) not petroleum/oil residues, and discard the first water if pitch or grease has fouled it.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Rebuilds/replaces the whole pump as the only fix while explicitly dismissing prime, suction-side air leaks, valves, and the leather seal as things to check.
Claims loss of prime indicates the well has run dry or the suction-lift limit was exceeded, when the pump worked fine the day before at the same depth.
reference_facts: Loss of prime in a suction pump is most commonly an air leak on the suction side (loose/cracked joints, cylinder) or a leaking/blocked foot valve that lets water drain back when idle.
Reduced output with maintained prime usually means a worn or shrunken piston cup leaking past the bore, or a partially blocked valve/strainer.
Dried leather seals can be restored by soaking and greasing (tallow/beeswax) to swell and re-seal; cracked or torn leather must be replaced.
A squeak typically signals dry, unlubricated leather-on-bore contact; running dry accelerates wear and should be corrected by greasing.
Correct diagnostic order is re-prime/retest, then suction-side leak check, then valves, then piston seal, before any rebuild.

title: Troubleshoot a rope-and-washer pump that drains back
scenario: **Situation:** A survivor built a rope pump (a loop of rope with rubber washers/discs running up inside a riser pipe) to raise water from a well.
**Problem:** Water comes up but most of it slips back down past the washers; output is poor.
**Resources:** Spare washer material, the rope, the riser pipe, a crank/wheel to drive the rope, basic tools.
prompt: I built a rope pump to draw water from our well: a rope with rubber washers spaced along it, running up inside a vertical pipe, driven by a wheel. It lifts some water but most of it drains right back down past the washers before reaching the top, so output is terrible. How do I diagnose whether it's the washer fit, the rope speed, or something else, and how do I fix it? Tell me what clearances and speed actually matter and how to test the fix.
rubric: Explains the mechanism: each washer traps a slug of water above it inside the riser pipe and lifts it; the seal is the close fit of washer to pipe bore, NOT suction.
Diagnosis 1 (most likely): washer-to-pipe clearance too large, so water leaks back past each washer; fix by matching washer diameter closely to the pipe inner diameter.
Diagnosis 2: rope/washer speed too low, so trapped slugs drain back during the slow lift; a minimum lifting speed is needed to keep the water column moving up faster than it can leak down.

States the trade-off: washers must be close-fitting enough to seal but not so tight they bind/wear fast or overload the drive; aim for a snug sliding fit (clearance on the order of a millimetre or less).
Checks for worn or torn washers and replaces split/undersized ones; uniform washer size is required.
Inspects the bottom guide/inlet block (the funnel/guide that recentres the rope entering the pipe) for wear, misalignment, or a rope not centred in the pipe, which lets water bypass.
Verifies the riser pipe is straight, smooth-bored and not oversized relative to the washers; a kinked or oversized pipe destroys the seal.
Checks washer spacing along the rope so consecutive water slugs are supported and the load is shared; too-wide spacing reduces continuity of the column.
Test procedure: run at increasing speed and measure output (litres collected per minute) to confirm flow rises and back-drain falls; compare before/after a washer change.
Notes the practical lift/speed limits: rope pumps work best on modest lifts and need the rope cranked at a steady brisk pace; flow scales with speed up to where leakage is overcome.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Claims the rope pump works by suction/vacuum rather than by close-fitting washers physically lifting water.
Advises making washers extremely loose, or removing them, as the way to improve flow.
reference_facts: A rope (rope-and-washer) pump lifts water as discs/washers drag slugs of water up a riser pipe; lift depends on the washer-to-pipe seal, not suction.
Excess washer-to-bore clearance is the primary cause of back-leakage; washers should be a snug sliding fit (clearance ~<=1 mm) to the pipe inner diameter.
Below a minimum rope speed the dynamic seal fails and water drains back; output rises with rope speed.
A worn or misaligned bottom guide block lets the rope ride off-centre, breaking the seal.
Washers too tight cause excess friction and rapid wear / drive overload; the design target is a balanced snug fit.

title: Assess and shore a cracked two-storey brick building
scenario: Survivor wants to reuse a two-storey brick building as a workshop in an area where aftershocks are possible.
Walls are cracked and part of the roof is damaged.
Available: timber, rope, wedges, basic hand tools; no heavy machinery.
prompt: I found a two-storey brick building in the ruins I want to use as a workshop, but the walls are cracked and part of the roof has come down. Aftershocks are still possible. How do I judge whether the ground floor is safe enough to occupy? If it needs propping, how do I use timber, rope and wedges to brace the walls? And what specific signs mean I should abandon it entirely and stay out?
rubric: States that unreinforced masonry is strong in compression but weak in tension and especially weak out-of-plane (walls toppling sideways), which is the dominant collapse mode in aftershocks.

Defines clear NO-GO signs that mean stay out: walls bulging/leaning out of plumb, walls separating from floors/roof, wide (>~5-10 mm) or actively widening/diagonal stair-step cracks, sagging floors, or fresh debris/dust falling.
Distinguishes harmless fine hairline/shrinkage cracks from structural cracks (diagonal cracks at openings, through-wall cracks, cracks that have moved since last seen).
Checks walls for plumb with a hanging plumb-bob/weighted string and flags any visible lean or out-of-plumb as a stop condition.
Inspects that floors and roof still bear properly on the walls (no slipped/rotted joist ends, no gaps), since loss of the diaphragm lets walls splay.

Establishes a barricaded no-go exclusion zone of at least the building height (the toppling 'fall zone'), assesses from outside first with a spotter and a clear escape route, and never works alone inside.
Braces leaning/cracked walls with raking timber shores (props) set on a firm footing/sole plate, tightened with wedges, bearing against a solid wall area, to push the wall back toward plumb.
Creates a continuous load path: shores must transfer load down to solid ground/firm footing (not soft fill or rubble) so the prop actually carries the wall.
Adds horizontal ties/cross-bracing (rope or timber) where possible to hold opposite walls together and resist out-of-plane splaying.
Includes monitoring: mark crack ends/widths (date them or fit a paper 'tell-tale' across the crack) and re-check; if cracks grow or the wall moves, evacuate and do not re-enter.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Declares the building safe to occupy despite bulging/out-of-plumb walls or widening cracks, or omits any no-go/abandon criteria.
Has people work inside or directly under damaged walls/roof during assessment with no exclusion zone, escape route, or spotter.
reference_facts: Unreinforced masonry resists compression well but is weak in tension and out-of-plane bending; out-of-plane wall collapse is the leading masonry failure in earthquakes.
Bulging or out-of-plumb walls, walls separating from floors/roof, and widening diagonal/stair-step cracks are indicators of imminent collapse - do not occupy.
Raking shores must bear on a firm sole plate/footing and form a continuous load path to solid ground.
A safe exclusion/fall zone is at least the height of the wall/building.
Crack tell-tales (marking/dating crack tips or bridging with paper/glass) detect ongoing movement.
Fine hairline shrinkage cracks are generally non-structural; through-wall, diagonal, and progressing cracks are structural warnings.

title: Build a 0.8 m dry-stone retaining wall that won't topple
scenario: **Situation:** A person is terracing a sloped garden to stop erosion. The retaining wall will be ~0.8 m high and 10 m long.
**Materials:** Stones of mixed size, gravel/rubble, clay/soil; hand tools. No cement.
**Constraint:** The wall holds back wet soil that drains poorly after rain.
prompt: I'm building a dry-stone retaining wall to terrace my garden: about 0.8 m high and 10 m long, holding back soil that gets very wet after rain. I have stones of mixed sizes, gravel and rubble, and clay, but no cement. Why do these walls topple over when the soil gets wet, and how do I stop that? Tell me how to prepare the base, how thick the wall should be at the bottom, what angle to lean it, how to handle the water behind it, and how to lay the stones so it holds together.
rubric: Identifies water/hydrostatic pressure behind the wall as a primary failure mode: saturated soil pushes far harder than dry soil, causing the wall to slide forward or overturn.
Specifies free-draining backfill behind the wall (gravel/rubble/crushed stone), NOT clay packed against the back of the wall, so water cannot build up pressure.
Specifies weep gaps / drainage at the base (open vertical joints or a drain run) so water collected behind the wall escapes to the front and doesn't pool.
Specifies a level, compacted foundation set into the ground (footing trench on firm subsoil, not on topsoil), with the largest flat stones as the base course.
Specifies the wall is thick at the base (~1/3 to 1/2 of its height, i.e. roughly 0.3–0.4 m for a 0.8 m wall) and battered (leaning back into the slope, ~1:6 to 1:10, ~5–10°).
Specifies laying stones as headers/stretchers that bond across the wall (long stones reaching back into the bank as 'deadmen'/tie-stones) and breaking joints so vertical cracks don't run up the face.
Specifies tilting each course slightly back into the hill and packing the interior with smaller 'hearting' stones so courses don't rock, rather than leaving voids.
Notes masonry/dry-stone is strong in compression but has no tensile/mortar strength, so stability comes from mass, friction, batter and interlock — not from binding.
Names a concrete acceptance check: each placed stone should not rock when stepped on/pushed, and the back-lean and base width should be checked with a plumb line/level as you build.
Gives an explicit stop/watch trigger, e.g. 'stop and rebuild the section if the face bulges, leans forward of plumb, or stones shift after rain', not a generic caution.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
States drainage is unnecessary, or packs impermeable clay directly behind the wall with no drainage/weep path.
Specifies a vertical or forward-leaning face, or a base no wider than the top, so the wall overturns under wet-soil pressure.
reference_facts: Saturated soil exerts much higher lateral (hydrostatic + earth) pressure than dry soil; drainage of backfill is the key control for retaining-wall stability.
Dry-stone retaining walls are battered back into the slope, typically ~1:6 to 1:10 (about 5–10° from vertical).
Base thickness is commonly ~1/3 to 1/2 of wall height; for an 0.8 m wall that is roughly 0.3–0.4 m at the base.
Free-draining gravel/rubble backfill plus weep paths relieve water pressure; clay backfill traps water and increases load.
Tie-stones/throughstones bonding across and into the bank, plus broken joints, prevent the face from separating and bulging.

title: Build a centrifugal governor to steady a waterwheel sawmill
scenario: Survivor's waterwheel drives a sawmill; it overspeeds when the saw is unloaded and bogs down when cutting.
They want automatic speed regulation by throttling the water supply.
Available: scrap metal, heavy weights (balls), hinges/pivots, a controllable water gate or sluice.
prompt: My waterwheel drives a sawmill. It races dangerously fast when the saw isn't cutting, then bogs down the instant I start a cut. I want it to hold a steady speed automatically by adjusting the water flow. I have scrap metal, heavy weights, hinges, and a sluice gate I can open and close. How do I build a mechanical governor that throttles the water based on shaft speed? Explain the mechanism, the feedback direction, how to set the target speed, and how to stop it surging up and down.
rubric: Identifies the device as a centrifugal (flyball / Watt) governor.
Describes a vertical spindle driven (via belt or gears) from the main shaft so the governor spins in proportion to wheel speed.
Mounts two equal weights (flyballs) on arms hinged to the top of the spinning spindle so they can swing outward and upward.
States the physics: faster rotation increases centrifugal force, so the balls fly outward and rise; slower rotation lets them drop inward.
Links the rising arms to a sliding collar/sleeve on the spindle that moves axially (up) as the balls rise.
Connects the sliding collar through a lever linkage to the water gate, arranged so rising collar CLOSES (reduces) the water and falling collar OPENS it.
States the negative-feedback loop explicitly: overspeed -> balls rise -> collar up -> gate closes -> wheel slows back toward setpoint (and the reverse on underspeed).
Explains how to set the target speed: adjust ball mass, arm length, or add a restoring spring; heavier balls/stiffer spring raise the governed speed.
Adds damping (friction pad or dashpot on the collar) to prevent 'hunting' (continuous surging above and below the target speed).
Includes a setup/verification step: run unloaded, adjust spring/weights until the gate settles at the desired steady rpm, then apply load and confirm it self-corrects.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Reverses the feedback direction (has overspeed OPEN the gate / increase water), which would run the wheel away.
Omits any speed-sensing element, describing only a fixed/manual gate that cannot respond automatically to shaft speed.
reference_facts: A centrifugal (Watt/flyball) governor uses spinning weights whose centrifugal force lifts them as speed rises, moving a sliding collar.
Correct feedback is negative: overspeed must reduce the driving input (close the water gate); reversing it causes runaway.

For an idealised conical-pendulum governor the cone height h (pivot-to-ball-plane drop) relates to angular speed by h = g/omega^2, so higher speed reduces h, the balls swing outward and upward, and the sliding collar rises.
Target speed is set by ball mass, arm geometry, and any restoring spring.
Damping (friction or a dashpot) is required to prevent hunting/oscillation.

title: Build a foot-powered (treadle) lift pump from wood and leather
scenario: Situation: A grower needs to lift water about 2 m from a stream into barrels for irrigation and wants leg power so hands stay free.
Resources: Seasoned straight timber, leather and cloth, wooden pegs and a few nails, beeswax/pitch for sealing, a length of bored-out log or reed for the cylinder/pipe.
Constraint: No metal valves and no metal cylinder available; everything must be made from the listed materials.
Goal: A reliable, repairable pump moving enough water for a small garden.
prompt: I need to lift water about 2 metres from a stream up into barrels for my garden, and I want to pump with my legs so my hands stay free. I have seasoned timber, leather and cloth, wooden pegs, a few nails, beeswax and pitch, and I can bore out a log to make a cylinder. I have no metal valves. How do I build a foot-treadle pump from these? Explain exactly how the two valves and the piston seal work and which way each valve must open, how to make the leather seal and cup, how to prime it, and roughly how much water I can expect per stroke. What's the highest I could ever lift water by suction this way, and what do I watch for if it stops pumping?
rubric: Specifies a piston/lift-pump arrangement with TWO one-way (check) valves: a fixed foot/inlet valve at the cylinder base and a moving valve in the piston, both flap/clack valves made of leather hinged over a hole.

States the correct valve sense: on the up-stroke the inlet (foot) valve opens and the piston valve closes (drawing water in below the piston); on the down-stroke the foot valve closes and the piston valve opens (water passes above the piston) so it is lifted out on the next stroke.

Describes a leather cup/disc seal on the piston that is slightly oversized and lubricated with wax/tallow, sealing against the bored cylinder wall; the cup faces so water pressure expands it against the wall.

States the atmospheric suction-lift limit: water cannot be lifted by suction more than ~10 m even theoretically (atmospheric pressure ~10.3 m of water at sea level), and practically only ~7-8 m, so the ~2 m lift here is well within limits.
Requires the suction (below-piston) side and cylinder to be airtight, and explains priming by filling the cylinder/pouring water in to wet the seals and expel air so suction can develop.

Connects the cylinder to a treadle: a foot lever/rocker linked by rod or rope to the piston so leg motion drives the up/down stroke, with a return (spring pole, counterweight, or springy sapling) for the non-powered stroke.
Seals joints and the cylinder with pitch/wax and tight wooden joinery; warns that a leaky cylinder wall or loose piston cup is the main cause of weak output.

Gives a sane flow estimate from swept volume x stroke rate (e.g. a ~75-100 mm bore x ~200 mm stroke is roughly 1-1.5 L/stroke; at a steady treadle cadence this yields on the order of tens of litres per minute) and sanity-checks it against garden needs.
Specifies a foot valve set below water level with a coarse screen/strainer (cloth) to keep grit out and stop the valve jamming.

Troubleshooting/stop check: if it loses prime or output falls, suspect air leaks on the suction side, a worn leather cup, or debris under a valve; re-prime, reseal, and replace the leather before continuing.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Claims the pump can lift water by suction from far more than ~10 m without flagging this as physically impossible.
Gets the valve operation backwards (e.g. both valves opening together, or the foot valve open on the down-stroke) so the described pump could not actually move water.
Recommends lead pipe/lead solder or salvaged fuel/pesticide containers for parts that touch the water.
reference_facts: Atmospheric pressure supports a column of water about 10.3 m at sea level; this is the absolute theoretical suction-lift limit, with ~7-8 m the practical maximum for a real pump due to valve losses, vapour pressure and leaks.

A lift/piston pump uses two check valves: a fixed inlet (foot) valve and a valve in the piston; on the up-stroke the foot valve opens and the piston valve closes, on the down-stroke the foot valve closes and the piston valve opens.
A leather cup seal is oriented so pressure forces its lip outward against the cylinder wall; it must be kept supple and lubricated with tallow/wax.
Priming a suction pump means filling it with water to wet seals and expel air so the pressure difference can lift water; an air leak on the suction side prevents prime.
Pump output per stroke equals piston area x stroke length minus slip; a ~75-100 mm bore with ~200 mm stroke displaces roughly 0.9-1.6 L per stroke.

title: Build a wooden hand piston pump for a 5 m well
scenario: Situation: A household needs to lift water from a hand-dug well about 5 m deep using a hand pump.
Resources: Straight seasoned timber (some borable to make a cylinder/riser), leather, beeswax/pitch, rope, augers and drilling tools, wooden pegs.
Constraint: Mostly wood and leather; few or no metal valves; pump must hold water between strokes.
Goal: A repairable hand pump delivering drinking/garden water.
prompt: I need to pump water from a hand-dug well about 5 metres deep. I have seasoned timber I can bore out, leather, beeswax and pitch, rope and an auger. How do I build a hand piston pump that pulls water up and stops it falling back down between strokes? Explain how the moving parts work, exactly which way each valve opens, where to mount the cylinder relative to the water, how to cut and fit the leather seals, how to prime it, and how to maintain it. Is 5 m within reach for a suction pump, and what do I watch for if it stops drawing?
rubric: Describes a lift (piston) pump: a bored cylinder containing a piston that moves up and down, with a foot valve at the cylinder base and a valve through the piston, both one-way leather flap/clack valves.

States the valve sense correctly: up-stroke opens the foot valve and closes the piston valve (water drawn up into the cylinder); down-stroke closes the foot valve and opens the piston valve (water passes above the piston); the closed foot valve is what stops water falling back between strokes.
States the suction-lift limit (~10.3 m theoretical, ~7-8 m practical) and confirms a 5 m lift is within reach for a suction pump PROVIDED the cylinder sits within that suction height of the water.

Mounts the cylinder so the foot valve is within suction range of the water surface (ideally the cylinder near/at the bottom with a riser pipe, or at the top only if the lift to the water is under ~7 m), with a foot valve and strainer at the well bottom.
Details the leather piston cup/seal: a slightly oversized leather cup or disc, kept supple and lubricated, sealing against the bored cylinder bore; explains how to cut, wet-form and fit it.
Requires an airtight cylinder, riser and joints sealed with pitch/wax and tight joinery, warning that any air leak on the suction side will stop it drawing.
Specifies priming by pouring water in from the top to wet seals and fill the cylinder so suction can begin, especially on first use or after standing.
Describes the handle/lever linkage (rocker arm) connecting hand motion to the piston rod, sized to give comfortable leverage.
