rubric: Explains the line of thrust is the locus of the resultant compressive force through the ring, and the arch is stable only if it stays inside the masonry.
States the middle-third rule: keeping the thrust within the central third of the section keeps the whole joint in compression with no tension.
Gives the maximum eccentricity as e <= t/6 = 300/6 = 50 mm from the centreline of the 300 mm ring.
Computes horizontal thrust H = wL^2/(8d) = 25 x 4^2 / (8 x 1.0) = 50 kN per metre width.
Computes vertical reaction at each abutment = wL/2 = 25 x 4 / 2 = 50 kN per metre width.
Gives the resultant springing thrust = sqrt(H^2 + V^2) ~ 70.7 kN/m at about 45 degrees to the horizontal.
Identifies abutment SLIDING (horizontal thrust overcoming friction/passive resistance) as one failure mode and adds mass/buttressing/batter to resist it.
Identifies abutment OVERTURNING or spreading (foundation rotation/settlement letting the springings move apart) as the second failure mode.
States centering is struck only after the mortar/ring can carry thrust, and ideally struck gradually/from the crown so the arch seats and the thrust line settles.

Names a collapse warning sign: cracking/opening of a joint on one face (a forming hinge) - typically a tension crack at intrados near crown or extrados near haunch - signalling the thrust line has reached the ring edge, versus uniform tight closed joints meaning healthy compression.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
States the kern/eccentricity limit as t/2 or t/3 from the centreline, or claims masonry arches reliably carry tension.
Advises striking the centering immediately after laying the last voussoir before any mortar set / before the ring can take thrust.
reference_facts: For a rectangular cross-section, the resultant must stay within the kern (middle third) to keep the whole section in compression; the limiting eccentricity is e = t/6.
A masonry arch carries load essentially in compression; tension causes joints to open and form hinges; four hinges turn the arch into a mechanism and it collapses.
Horizontal thrust of an arch under uniform load approximates H = wL^2/(8d) where d is the rise (same form as a cable/parabola).
For w=25 kN/m, L=4 m, d=1 m: H=50 kN/m, V=wL/2=50 kN/m, resultant ~70.7 kN/m at ~45 degrees.
Abutments must resist both the outward horizontal thrust (sliding/spreading) and the overturning tendency; insufficient abutment is the classic arch failure.
Centering should be struck gently after the ring can sustain its thrust line, allowing the arch to deflect slightly and seat into compression.

title: Compare: single-log beam bridge vs cable suspension footbridge over a 15 m ravine
scenario: A settlement needs a foot crossing over a 15 m wide ravine with steep banks; the gap is too wide to wade or jump.
Available: several straight logs up to ~6 m, a length of strong steel cable plus some rope, hand tools, and the ability to dig anchor pits and set timber.
The asker wants to choose between a beam bridge and a suspension bridge and understand the trade-offs and safety limits.
prompt: We need a foot crossing over a ravine about 15 metres wide with steep sides. We have several straight logs up to about 6 m long, a coil of strong steel cable and some rope, hand tools, and we can dig pits to anchor things. Should we lay a log across as a beam bridge or build a cable suspension footbridge? Compare the two for this 15 m gap, explain the trade-offs and what each one needs to be safe, and tell me which to build and why.
rubric: States that a single unsupported timber beam is span-limited (realistically only about 5–6 m clear span for foot traffic), so a 15 m clear span exceeds what one of the available logs can safely carry.

Explains a beam carries load in bending and that bending stress and deflection rise steeply with span (bending moment scales with span, e.g. roughly with its square for a distributed load), so a 15 m log would sag and likely fail.
Explains a suspension/cable bridge carries the deck load as TENSION in the cable, which is structurally efficient and allows far longer spans than a beam.

States the suspension design transfers all load to its anchorages, so it needs strong, deeply buried deadman anchors (or anchoring to bedrock/large living trees) able to resist the full cable tension.
Notes the suspension cable must be allowed to sag, because a flatter (tighter) cable carries dramatically higher tension for the same load — over-tensioning can overload the cable and anchors.
Identifies the beam bridge's advantages: simple, stiff (little sway or bounce), fast to build, needs no special hardware, and easy to make safe over a SHORT span.
Identifies the suspension bridge's disadvantages: it sways and bounces, demands strong cable and connection hardware, needs more skill, and an anchorage failure is sudden and catastrophic.
Recommends, for the 15 m clear gap, either a cable/suspension footbridge OR splitting the span with an intermediate pier/trestle into two short (<6 m) beam spans — not a single unsupported log.
Includes a safety check before use: proof-load the finished bridge and limit crossings to one person at a time, with a clear margin over expected load.

Justifies the final choice by matching it to the actual materials (strong cable + good anchors favours suspension; ability to set a mid-ravine support favours a piered beam) rather than by preference alone.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the comparison.
Recommends spanning the full 15 m with a single unsupported log or timber beam as if it would be safe to walk across.
Claims a tighter, sag-free suspension cable is safer or lower-stress than a sagging one (it is the opposite).
reference_facts: A simple timber beam footbridge is practically limited to roughly 5–6 m clear span; a 15 m clear span requires a suspension/cable structure or an intermediate support (pier/trestle).
A beam carries load in bending; maximum bending moment for a central point load is PL/4 and for a uniform load wL²/8, so stress and deflection rise steeply with span L.
A suspension bridge carries deck load as cable tension transferred to anchorages; a flatter (less-sagging) cable carries much higher tension for the same load.
Anchorages (deadman anchors, or anchoring to bedrock/large trees) must resist the full cable tension; anchorage failure causes total, sudden collapse.
A light improvised footbridge should be proof-loaded before use and limited to one person at a time.

title: Compare: lime mortar vs Portland-cement mortar for rebuilding a soft-stone wall
scenario: A group is rebuilding a wall of soft, porous local stone and must choose a mortar; they can fire a simple wood/charcoal kiln but have no industrial cement plant.
They have a small remaining stock of bagged Portland cement and the ability to burn local limestone for lime.
The asker wants to understand the trade-offs between lime mortar and cement mortar for this soft stone, and what is realistically producible.
prompt: We are rebuilding a wall made of soft, porous stone. We have a little bagged Portland cement left, and we can also burn local limestone in a simple kiln to make lime. Should we point and bed the stone with lime mortar or with Portland-cement mortar? Compare them for soft stone — strength, how they set, breathability, how each ages, and which we can actually keep making ourselves — and tell me which to use.
rubric: Explains non-hydraulic lime mortar sets and hardens by carbonation (slowly reabsorbing CO₂ from the air), curing over weeks–months rather than hours.
Explains Portland-cement mortar sets by hydration (reaction with water), gaining strength in hours–days and reaching far higher compressive strength than lime.
States lime mortar is vapour-permeable ('breathable') and lets a wall dry out, whereas cement mortar is dense and largely impermeable and can trap moisture in the stone.

Explains that on soft/porous stone a hard, impermeable cement mortar concentrates stress and traps water and salts, causing the STONE face (not the mortar) to spall and crack — the mortar should be weaker than the stone.
Notes lime mortar is softer and slightly flexible, accommodates small movement, can self-heal hairline cracks, and is the 'sacrificial' element that is easier to rake out and repoint.

Compares manufacturability: quicklime is made by burning limestone at roughly 900 °C (achievable in a simple wood/charcoal kiln), whereas Portland clinker needs ~1400–1450 °C, which is very hard to reach without an industrial kiln.
Identifies cement's genuine advantages: high early and ultimate strength, fast set, and good performance in wet, heavily loaded, or freezing conditions where slow lime is unsuitable.
Notes lime mortar's weaknesses: slow set, low strength, and vulnerability to frost/rain before it has carbonated, so it is poor for heavy structural loads or constant saturation.

Recommends matching mortar to the situation — for a soft-stone wall with only simple kilns available, a lime mortar (ideally a hydraulic lime) is the better, breathable, producible choice; reserve scarce cement for high-strength or wet structural work.

Gives workable mix/curing guidance, e.g. lime mortar around 1 part lime to ~2.5–3 parts sand, kept damp and protected from rain and frost while it slowly cures; do not seal soft stone with a strong cement render.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the comparison.
Claims lime mortar is as strong as, or stronger than, Portland-cement mortar in compression.
Claims Portland cement can be produced in an ordinary wood fire or lime kiln at lime-burning temperatures (it requires ~1400–1450 °C).
reference_facts: Non-hydraulic lime mortar sets by carbonation (slowly absorbing atmospheric CO₂): breathable, slightly flexible, low-strength, self-heals fine cracks. Hydraulic lime sets faster with some water reaction.
Portland-cement mortar sets by hydration in hours–days and is much stronger and more water-resistant, but is rigid, brittle, and largely impermeable.
Quicklime (CaO) is made by calcining limestone (CaCO₃) at roughly 900 °C; Portland clinker requires ~1400–1450 °C.
On soft/porous stone the mortar should be weaker and more permeable than the stone; a hard impermeable cement mortar traps moisture and salts and causes the stone face to spall.
Typical lime mortar mix ≈ 1 part lime : 2.5–3 parts sand; protect from rain and frost during the slow cure.

title: Compare: gravity-fed spring line vs rope pump on a well for settlement water
scenario: A settlement has a spring on a hillside above the camp and a hand-dug well within the camp, and must choose how to deliver water reliably.
Available: pipe/hose for a gravity line, materials to build a rope pump on the well, and labour; no grid power or fuel for pumping.
The asker wants to weigh a gravity-fed line from the spring against a rope pump on the well.
prompt: We have a spring uphill above our camp and a hand-dug well inside the camp. We have pipe and hose, and materials to build a rope pump, but no electricity or fuel for pumping. Should we run a gravity-fed line down from the spring or put a rope pump on the well? Compare the two — effort, reliability, what each needs to work, and seasonal risk — and tell me which to rely on.
rubric: Identifies the key requirement for the gravity system: the spring must be reliably higher than the delivery point, and notes that more vertical drop (head) gives more pressure and flow.
Notes a gravity-fed line needs no fuel or muscle once built — water flows continuously by itself day and night — making it the low-effort option.
States the gravity line depends on the spring's flow and can weaken or fail in dry seasons, and that the pipe must run continuously downhill with no high points that trap air and cause an airlock.
States the spring intake must be protected (a spring box or screened intake) to keep out debris, animals, and contamination, and the line shaded/buried to limit algae or freezing.
Explains the rope pump lifts water by human muscle power, so it works on a well with no usable elevation and is independent of any uphill source.
Notes the rope pump's downside: it delivers water only while someone is actively pumping (or fills a tank in batches) and so requires continuous human effort.
Compares maintenance: the rope pump has wearing moving parts (rope, pistons/seals, wheel) that need periodic replacement, while the gravity line is mostly passive but can clog, leak, or airlock.
Recommends pairing either source with a storage/header tank so supply is buffered against demand peaks, pump downtime, or variable spring flow.

Recommends the gravity-fed spring as the primary supply where head and flow are sufficient (lowest ongoing effort), keeping the well and rope pump as backup for dry seasons or if the spring is contaminated.
Adds a water-safety note: even a protected spring should be treated/disinfected if contamination is possible — a protected source reduces but does not guarantee microbiological safety.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the comparison.
Claims gravity flow can move water uphill from a lower source to a higher tank without any pump.
reference_facts: Gravity-fed flow requires the source to be higher than the delivery point; available pressure (head) equals the vertical drop, and the line must slope continuously downward to avoid airlocks.
A gravity system needs no power once built but depends on the source flow, which can fall in dry seasons.
A rope pump raises water by human power, works on a well with no elevation, flows only while pumped, and has wearing parts (rope, seals).
Spring intakes need a protected, screened spring box; even spring water should be treated if contamination is possible.
A storage/header tank buffers supply against demand peaks and source/pump interruptions.

title: Investigate: a hand pump that suddenly stopped delivering water
scenario: A hand pump on a well has suddenly stopped bringing up water — the handle still moves but little or nothing comes out.
It is the settlement's main water lift.
The asker wants to diagnose and fix it.
prompt: Our hand pump on the well suddenly stopped bringing up water — the handle still moves but little or no water comes out. How do I work out what is wrong and fix it? Walk me through the likely causes and how to check each one in order.
rubric: Checks the likely loss-of-prime cause first: a suction pump needs water in the cylinder to seal, so re-prime by pouring water in and pumping to see if it then draws.

Explains a suction/lift pump works by atmospheric pressure pushing water up into the low pressure made by the piston, which limits suction lift to roughly 7–8 m; if the water is deeper than that the pump cannot lift it.
Checks the well's water level: the water table may have fallen (drought/over-pumping) below the pump intake or below the suction-lift limit.
Suspects worn piston cup seals/leathers or a worn cylinder: without a seal the pump can't build suction — the handle moves with little resistance and no water comes.
Suspects a leaking foot/check valve: if the foot valve at the bottom or the piston check valve leaks, water drains back and the pump loses its prime between strokes.
Checks for an air leak in the suction/rising main: a loose joint, crack, or pinhole above the water lets the pump suck air instead of water (little handle resistance).
Checks for a blockage: a clogged intake screen, debris, or a pipe frozen in cold weather stopping flow.

Gives a logical diagnostic sequence: re-prime first; if it draws then loses it, look at valves/leaks; if it never draws and the handle is loose, look at seals/air leaks; if nothing and the well seems dry, check the water level.
Gives the fixes: replace worn cup seals/leathers, reseat or replace valves, seal/repair leaking joints in the suction line, clear blockages, and lower/deepen the cylinder if the water table dropped.
Notes a deep-well pump with the cylinder down in the water (rather than a surface suction pump) avoids the suction-lift limit, and that keeping spare leathers/valves allows quick repair.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the investigation.
Claims a surface suction/lift pump can draw water up from far deeper than ~7–8 m, ignoring the atmospheric-pressure limit, and so misdiagnoses the fault.
reference_facts: A suction/lift pump relies on atmospheric pressure pushing water up into the low pressure made by the piston; practical suction lift is limited to roughly 7–8 m (theoretical ~10 m at sea level).

Common faults: lost prime (re-prime by pouring water in), worn piston cup seals/leathers, leaking foot/check valves (water drains back), air leaks in the suction line, clogged intake, or a frozen pipe.
A dropped water table (drought/over-pumping) below the intake or below the suction-lift limit stops a surface pump from lifting water.
Diagnose by re-priming first, then distinguishing draws-then-loses-it (valves/leaks) from handle-loose-never-draws (seals/air leak) from low well water level.

Fixes: replace seals/leathers, reseat/replace valves, seal suction-line leaks, clear blockages, lower/deepen the cylinder; a deep-well pump with the cylinder in the water avoids the suction-lift limit.

title: Investigate: a dug well's water turned muddy and the level dropped
scenario: A hand-dug well's water has turned muddy and cloudy and the level has dropped.
It could be running dry, collapsing, or being contaminated.
The asker wants to find the cause and protect the supply and the people drinking from it.
prompt: Our hand-dug well's water has turned muddy and cloudy and the level has dropped. How do I figure out what is going on — is it running dry, collapsing, or getting contaminated — and what should we do, including whether it is safe to drink?
rubric: Lays out the main possibilities to investigate: a falling water table (drought/over-use), wall/lining collapse stirring up sediment, surface water/contamination getting in, or over-pumping drawing in silt.
Gives the falling-water-table clue: the level dropping over a dry season or with heavy use, and neighbouring wells/springs also low — pointing to drawdown rather than a well fault.
Gives the collapse/caving clue: sudden muddiness with debris, the bottom filling in (shallower depth), or visible damage in an unlined/poorly lined well.
Gives the contamination-ingress clue: cloudiness after rain, runoff, or flooding, or a nearby latrine/animal area draining toward the well — surface water entering, which is also a health hazard.
Gives the over-pumping/silt clue: the water clears when rested and clouds when drawn hard/fast — the well is pumped faster than it recharges, pulling in fines.
Recommends letting the well rest and observing whether the level recovers and the water clears, to separate temporary drawdown from a structural or contamination problem.
Advises improving the wellhead: a raised, sealed headwall, a cover, and a drainage apron sloping away to keep surface water, runoff, and debris out.
Stresses the water-safety implication: muddy/contaminated well water should be treated (settle/filter, then boil or chlorinate) before drinking until the cause is fixed.

Recommends physical fixes per cause: line/stabilise the walls if collapsing, deepen the well or lower the intake if the table dropped, divert surface drainage and seal the head if contamination, reduce pumping rate or add storage if over-pumping.
Advises checking for nearby contamination sources (latrines, animal pens, waste) and keeping them at a safe distance and down-gradient from the well.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the investigation.
Advises drinking the muddy/cloudy well water untreated, ignoring the contamination and health risk.
reference_facts: Causes of a muddy, dropping dug well: falling water table (drought/over-use), wall/lining collapse stirring sediment, surface-water/contamination ingress, or over-pumping pulling in silt.

Falling-table clue: area-wide low water; collapse clue: sudden debris and the bottom filling in; contamination clue: cloudiness after rain/runoff near latrines/animals; over-pumping clue: clears when rested, clouds when drawn hard.
Let the well rest to see if it recovers/clears, separating temporary drawdown from structural/contamination problems.
Protect the wellhead: raised sealed headwall, cover, and an apron draining away; keep latrines/animal pens well away and down-gradient.
Treat muddy/contaminated well water (settle/filter, then boil or chlorinate) before drinking until fixed; line walls, deepen, divert drainage, or reduce pumping per the diagnosis.

title: Investigate: a stove and chimney that smoke back into the room
scenario: A newly built stove and chimney smoke back into the room instead of drawing the smoke up and out.
The space is used for heating and cooking.
The asker wants to find why it will not draw and fix it.
prompt: We built a stove and chimney, but it smokes back into the room instead of drawing the smoke up and out. How do I work out why it will not draw properly, and how do I fix it? Is the smoke itself a danger?
rubric: Explains chimney draft is driven by the stack effect: hot flue gas is less dense than outside air, so a taller, hotter, well-sealed flue pulls harder, while a cold, short, or leaky flue draws poorly.
Checks flue height/termination: a flue that is too short, or whose top is not above the roof ridge and nearby obstructions, draws poorly and is prone to wind downdraft.

Checks for a cold flue / cold start: a cold chimney (especially an exterior one) can hold a plug of cold dense air that reverses flow; warming the flue first (burning a twist of paper/kindling) primes the draft.
Checks for blockage: a bird/nest, debris, soot/creosote build-up, a closed or stuck damper, or a collapsed liner restricting the flue.
Checks flue cross-section: a flue too large for the appliance cools and stalls, too small cannot carry the volume — it should roughly match the stove outlet.
Checks for air leaks or a competing opening on the same chimney (a second appliance/damper), which let the draft short-circuit; the flue must be sealed.

Checks the combustion-air supply: a tightly sealed room, an extractor fan, or another fire competing for air creates negative pressure that overpowers the chimney — crack a window or provide an air inlet.
Checks for wind downdraft from nearby taller structures or trees pushing air back down the flue (a cap/cowl or raising the flue can help).
Recommends a logical test sequence: prime a cold flue, check for blockage and damper, check height/cap and competing exhaust/air supply, seal leaks — changing one thing at a time.

Gives fixes (raise the flue above the ridge, fit a cap/cowl, clear blockages, right-size and seal the flue, provide combustion air, prime a cold flue) and warns that a back-smoking stove fills the room with dangerous carbon monoxide until it is fixed.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the investigation.
Ignores the carbon-monoxide hazard of a back-drafting stove, or advises continuing to run it in an unventilated room.
reference_facts: Chimney draft comes from the stack effect: hot, less-dense flue gas rises, so a taller, hotter, well-sealed flue draws harder; cold/short/leaky flues draw poorly.

Poor-draft causes: flue too short or not above the ridge/obstructions, a cold flue (cold-air plug — prime it), blockage (nest/debris/soot/closed damper), wrong flue size, air leaks or a competing opening on the flue, insufficient combustion air (sealed room/extractor fan), and wind downdraft.
Flue cross-section should match the appliance outlet; too large cools and stalls, too small cannot carry the volume.
Fixes: raise/extend the flue above the ridge, fit a cap/cowl, clear blockages, right-size and seal the flue, provide combustion air, and prime a cold flue before lighting.
A back-drafting/smoking stove fills the room with smoke and carbon monoxide — a serious hazard until corrected; ensure ventilation.

title: Lesson plan: teaching beginners to lay a straight, level, bonded brick wall
scenario: Beginners must learn to lay bricks or blocks into a sound wall with mortar.
Tools (string line, level, trowel) and mortar materials are available.
The asker wants a hands-on lesson covering technique and the checks that keep a wall true and strong.
prompt: I need to teach beginners to lay bricks into a straight, strong wall with mortar. Design a hands-on lesson covering how to start, how to lay and bed the bricks, how to keep the wall straight, level and upright, and what makes the wall strong rather than weak. Include how I will check they have got it.
rubric: States the objective: learners can lay bricks/blocks into a straight, level, plumb, well-bonded wall.
Teaches starting from a solid, level foundation/footing, since errors at the base carry up the whole wall.
Teaches mixing mortar to a workable consistency and laying a full mortar bed plus buttering the head (end) joints.
Teaches laying to a stretched string line and checking each course/brick for LEVEL (horizontal) and PLUMB (vertical) with a spirit level and/or plumb line.
Teaches staggering the vertical joints between courses (bonding/overlapping, e.g. by half a brick) so joints do not line up — which is what gives the wall its strength.
Teaches consistent joint thickness, tapping each brick down to the line, and striking/pointing the joints.
Teaches not building too high too fast — letting the mortar set so the wall does not slump or topple — and keeping courses level as you go.
Teaches building and plumbing the corners/ends (leads) first and filling between them to a line, keeping corners square.
Uses demonstrate → guided → independent hands-on practice with feedback.
Checks competence by having learners build a short section that is straight, level, plumb, and properly bonded.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting a usable lesson plan.
Teaches stacking bricks with the vertical joints lined up (no bond/overlap) as acceptable, or presents an unchecked crooked/out-of-plumb wall as structurally sound.
reference_facts: Bricklaying starts from a solid, level foundation; base errors carry up the whole wall.
Use a full mortar bed and buttered head joints; lay to a stretched string line and check each course for level (horizontal) and plumb (vertical).
Stagger/overlap vertical joints between courses (bonding, e.g. half-brick offset) so joints do not align — this gives the wall its strength.
Keep joint thickness consistent, tap bricks down to the line, and point the joints; do not build too high too fast or the mortar slumps.
Build and plumb the corners (leads) first and fill between to a line; assess with a short straight, level, plumb, well-bonded section.

title: Lesson plan: teaching safe tree felling and firewood cutting with hand tools
scenario: People must fell small trees and cut/split firewood with axes and hand saws; this is a major injury risk.
Tools and protective gear are limited.
The asker wants a lesson built around doing it without serious injury.
prompt: I need to teach people to fell small trees and cut and split firewood using axes and hand saws. This scares me because of the injury risk. Design a lesson that teaches them to do it safely — how to plan the fall, the cuts that control where a tree goes, how to stay out of harm's way, and safe cutting and splitting — and how to gate the dangerous parts until they are ready.
rubric: States the objective: learners can fell a small tree and cut/split firewood with hand tools without injuring themselves or others.
Teaches the ground rules: never work alone, keep bystanders well clear, wear what protection is available, and use sharp, sound tools with secure footing.
Teaches pre-felling assessment: the tree's lean and weight, the wind, dead or hanging limbs ('widowmakers') overhead, the surroundings, and the intended fall direction.
Teaches planning and clearing two escape routes (roughly 45° back from the planned fall direction) BEFORE cutting.

Teaches the felling cuts: a notch/face cut on the side the tree should fall toward, then a back cut on the opposite side slightly above the notch, leaving an uncut 'hinge' of wood to control the fall.
Teaches using wedges if needed and never cutting all the way through, and watching the top of the tree as it begins to move.
Teaches retreating along an escape route as the tree falls and watching for kickback, the butt jumping back, and falling limbs.
Teaches safe limbing and cross-cutting (bucking) — wary of the log rolling or the saw/axe binding — and safe splitting on a chopping block with a clear swing arc.
Uses demonstrate → supervised practice and gates actual felling until basic tool handling is shown.
Reinforces stopping if conditions (wind, fatigue, a hung-up tree) become unsafe, and checks competence before unsupervised work.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting a usable lesson plan.
Tells learners to fell a tree by cutting straight through with no notch/hinge and no escape route, or to stand/work directly below others felling or limbing.
reference_facts: Tree felling is high-risk: never work alone, keep bystanders clear, use sharp sound tools and secure footing, and watch for overhead dead/hanging limbs ('widowmakers').
Assess lean, weight, wind, and surroundings; plan the fall direction and clear two escape routes ~45° back from it before cutting.
Cut a notch/face cut on the fall side, then a back cut slightly above it on the opposite side, leaving an uncut 'hinge' to control the fall; never cut fully through; use wedges as needed.
Retreat along an escape route as it falls; watch for kickback, the butt jumping, and falling limbs; limb/buck mindful of rolling logs and a binding saw.
Split on a chopping block with a clear swing arc; gate live felling until basic tool handling is shown; stop if wind/fatigue/a hung-up tree make it unsafe.

title: Troubleshoot: a treadle-machine drive belt that keeps slipping
scenario: A treadle- or hand-powered machine's drive belt keeps slipping, so power is lost and the machine stalls under load.
Tools and replacement belt material are limited.
The asker wants to find and fix the cause of the slipping.
prompt: The drive belt on our treadle-powered machine keeps slipping, so it loses power and stalls when I put any load on it. How do I work out why it is slipping and fix it? Give me what to check and in what order.
rubric: Identifies insufficient belt TENSION as the most common cause and the first thing to check and adjust.
Checks the belt and pulley surfaces for wear, glazing (a shiny hard surface), or oil/grease contamination that reduce grip.
Checks pulley ALIGNMENT: misaligned or wobbling pulleys make the belt ride wrong and slip or come off.
Notes OVERLOADING: if the driven load is too heavy the belt slips, so reduce the load or increase tension/contact.
Checks the belt type/fit: a flat or round belt grips less than a V-belt seated in a matching groove, so the right belt and groove fit matter.
Checks the wrap/contact angle around the pulley: too little belt wrapped on a small pulley reduces grip, and an idler can increase the wrap.
Gives grip fixes: clean and degrease the surfaces, lightly roughen a glazed belt/pulley, and apply rosin or a grip compound as a temporary aid.
Gives the tensioning fix: a tensioner/idler pulley or moving the driven unit to take up slack, tensioned firm but not so tight it damages bearings.
Recommends replacing a stretched, cracked, or worn belt rather than over-tightening a bad one.
Recommends a logical order — check tension and contamination first, then alignment, wear, and load — changing one thing at a time and testing.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting to troubleshoot.
Advises loosening the belt to cure slipping (which makes it worse).
reference_facts: Belt slipping is most often insufficient tension, or glazed/worn/oil-contaminated belt or pulley surfaces, or pulley misalignment.
Grip also depends on belt type/fit (a V-belt seated in a matching groove grips far better than a flat/round belt) and the wrap/contact angle around the pulley.
Overloading the driven machine causes slip; reduce the load or increase tension/contact.

Fixes: clean/degrease and lightly roughen glazed surfaces, apply rosin as a temporary grip aid, align pulleys, add an idler to increase wrap/tension, and tension firm (not so tight it damages bearings).
Replace a stretched/cracked/worn belt rather than over-tightening it; loosening a belt makes slipping worse.

title: Troubleshoot: a hand grain mill that jams or grinds unevenly
scenario: A hand-cranked burr/stone grain mill keeps jamming or produces coarse, uneven flour.
Grinding grain is essential for the group's food.
The asker wants to diagnose and fix it.
prompt: Our hand-cranked grain mill keeps jamming, and when it does grind, the flour comes out coarse and uneven. How do I work out what is wrong and fix it? Tell me what to check and how to prevent it happening again.
rubric: Checks the grinding GAP/adjustment first: too tight binds/jams and overheats, too loose gives coarse uneven flour — adjust to the desired fineness.
Checks for foreign objects (stones, metal) in the grain jamming or damaging the burrs/stones, and stresses cleaning the grain before milling.
Checks the burrs/stones for wear, dullness, or damage that gives uneven grinding, and dresses/sharpens or replaces them.
Checks the FEED rate: feeding grain too fast overwhelms and jams the mill, so feed steadily and slowly.
Checks grain MOISTURE: damp grain gums up and clogs the mill, so grain should be dry (or ground in small amounts).
Checks alignment/seating of the burrs or plates and that they are not loose or skewed, which causes an uneven gap and grind.
Checks for build-up/clogging of flour or chaff between the surfaces and clears it.
Notes overheating from over-tight or too-fast grinding can scorch the flour and damage the mill — let it cool and lighten the cut.
Recommends a logical sequence: stop and clear any jam safely, check for foreign objects, then the gap, then feed/moisture, then burr condition.
Recommends preventive practice: clean and dry the grain, grind in passes (coarse then fine) rather than forcing one tight pass, and maintain/clean the burrs.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting to troubleshoot.
Advises forcing or hammering the mill through a jam without first clearing the obstruction, or ignores removing foreign objects from the grain.
reference_facts: Hand burr/stone mill problems: gap too tight (jams/overheats) or too loose (coarse/uneven); foreign objects (stones) jamming or damaging burrs; dull/worn/misaligned burrs giving uneven grind.
Feeding too fast jams the mill; damp grain gums and clogs it — grain should be dry and fed steadily.
Clear build-up of flour/chaff between the surfaces; overheating from too-tight/too-fast grinding scorches flour and damages the mill.
Fixes: adjust the gap, clean grain and remove foreign objects, dress/sharpen or replace burrs, check alignment and seating, slow the feed, and dry the grain.
Grind in passes (coarse then fine) rather than forcing one tight pass; clear jams by stopping and removing the obstruction, not by forcing it.
